stem: A rectangle has length 8 and width 5. What is its area?
solutionStandard: \(A = l \times w = 8 \times 5 = 40\).

stem: A square has a perimeter of 24. What is its area?
A parallelogram has a base of 10 and a height of 6. What is its area?
solutionStandard: \(A = \text{base} \times \text{height} = 10 \times 6 = 60\).

stem: A square and a rectangle each have a perimeter of 20. The rectangle has length 7 and width 3.
solutionStandard: Square side \(= 20/4 = 5\), area \(= 25\). Rectangle area \(= 7 \times 3 = 21\). Since \(25 > 21\), Quantity A is greater.
solutionShortcut: For a fixed perimeter, a square always encloses more area than any other rectangle — no computation needed to know A is greater, only to confirm by how much.

stem: A trapezoid has parallel sides of length 8 and 12, and a height of 5. What is its area?
Which of the following are true for every parallelogram? Select all that apply.

options.text: Opposite sides are equal in length.
The diagonals are equal in length.
All four angles are right angles.

solutionStandard: Equal opposite sides, equal opposite angles, and diagonals bisecting each other hold for every parallelogram. Equal diagonals (B) and right angles (E) hold only for rectangles (and squares), which are special cases.

stem: A rectangle has a diagonal of length 13 and one side of length 5. What is the length of the other side?
solutionStandard: The diagonal, together with the two sides, forms a right triangle: \(5^2 + b^2 = 13^2\), so \(b^2 = 144\), and \(b = 12\).

stem: A rectangular garden has a length twice its width. If the perimeter is 60, what is the area of the garden?
solutionStandard: Let width \(= w\), length \(= 2w\). Perimeter: \(2(w + 2w) = 60\), so \(6w = 60\) and \(w = 10\). Length \(= 20\). Area \(= 10 \times 20 = 200\).

stem: \(ABCD\) is a quadrilateral (any four-sided figure).
quantityA: The sum of the interior angles of \(ABCD\)
solutionStandard: The interior angles of any quadrilateral always sum to \(360^\circ\), regardless of its specific shape. The quantities are equal.

stem: A square has an area of 49. What is the length of its diagonal?
solutionStandard: Side \(= \sqrt{49} = 7\). The diagonal of a square with side \(s\) is \(s\sqrt{2}\), so the diagonal is \(7\sqrt{2}\).

stem: In the figure, what is the area of the rectangle?
solutionStandard: \(A = l \times w = {{l}} \times {{w}} = {{l*w}}\).

stem: A square has a perimeter of 32. What is its area?
A parallelogram has a base of {{b}} and a height of {{h}}. What is its area?
solutionStandard: \(A = \text{base} \times \text{height} = {{b}} \times {{h}} = {{b*h}}\).

stem: A square and a rectangle each have a perimeter of {{4*s}}. The rectangle has length {{s+d}} and width {{s-d}}.
solutionStandard: Square area \(={{s}}^2\). Rectangle area \(=({{s}}+{{d}})({{s}}-{{d}})={{s}}^2-{{d}}^2\), always less than the square's area whenever \({{d}} \neq 0\). Quantity A is greater.
solutionShortcut: For a fixed perimeter, a square always encloses the most area among all rectangles — no computation needed.

stem: A trapezoid has parallel sides of length {{a}} and {{b}}, and a height of {{h}}. What is its area?
Which of the following are true for every rectangle? Select all that apply.

options.text: The diagonals are equal in length.
All four angles are right angles.
All four sides are equal in length.

solutionStandard: Equal diagonals, diagonals bisecting each other, and four right angles hold for every rectangle. Equal side lengths (D) hold only for the special case of a square.

stem: A rectangle has a diagonal of length {{13*k}} and one side of length {{5*k}}. What is the length of the other side?
solutionStandard: This is the 5-12-13 triple scaled by \({{k}}\): the other side is \({{12*k}}\).

stem: A rectangular garden has a length twice its width. If the perimeter is {{6*w}}, what is the area of the garden?
solutionStandard: With width \(w\) and length \(2w\), perimeter \(=6w={{6*w}}\), so \(w={{w}}\) and length \(={{2*w}}\). Area \(={{w}}\times{{2*w}}={{2*w*w}}\).

stem: \(ABCD\) is a parallelogram whose diagonals intersect at point \(P\).
solutionStandard: The diagonals of any parallelogram bisect each other, so \(P\) is always the midpoint of diagonal \(AC\), making \(PA=PC\). The quantities are always equal.

stem: A rectangle has a diagonal of length 10 and a perimeter of 28. What is its area?
solutionStandard: Let the sides be \(l\) and \(w\). \(l+w=14\) (half the perimeter) and \(l^2+w^2=100\) (diagonal squared). Since \((l+w)^2 = l^2+2lw+w^2\), \(196 = 100+2lw\), giving \(2lw=96\) and the area \(lw=48\).
